Glossary About
Early Modern

David Hume

1711 – 1776

A bundle of perceptions: the self as habit, and the limits of introspection.

The most faithful way to extract a philosophy of mind from Hume is not to ask, “What metaphysical substance is the mind?” but rather, “What can be known, described, explained, inferred, and responsibly imagined about mindedness under the constraints of experience?” Hume’s answer is severe but not shallow. He does not simply replace the soul with matter, nor does he merely dismiss theology as superstition. He constructs a “science of man” whose proper object is the operation of the mind: perception, belief, association, passion, will, memory, identity, sympathy, agency, and the formation of ideas of other minds, including superior, divine, and invisible minds. Hume’s philosophy of mind is therefore empirical, phenomenological, skeptical, affective, social, and analogical.

The central thesis can be stated as follows: for Hume, the mind is not known as a simple, identical, immaterial substance standing behind experience. It is known as a system, succession, or “republic” of perceptions, linked by associative, causal, memorial, passionate, and social relations. The soul as substratum is unintelligible to natural reason; the soul as the living order of consciousness, passion, belief, agency, and personal concern is the proper subject of philosophy. Claims about God, angels, and superior spirits are not irrelevant to this project. They test the upper limits of Hume’s theory of ideas, analogy, causation, personhood, and other minds.

1. Hume’s method: a science of mind without a metaphysics of mental essence

Hume begins from the claim that philosophy must be grounded in a “science of man.” The human understanding is not merely one topic among others; it is the tribunal through which all other inquiries pass. He says that no question of importance can be decided before the science of man is placed on a proper foundation, and that this foundation must be “experience and observation.” At the same time, he sharply limits what such inquiry can reach: the “essence of the mind” is as unknown as the essence of external bodies, and philosophy must not pretend to discover ultimate original qualities of the soul beyond what experience can warrant.

This methodological point is decisive. Hume’s philosophy of mind is not a doctrine of what mind is in itself. It is an account of mental operations as they appear, combine, influence conduct, and generate belief. He does not say there is no mind, no soul, or no consciousness. Rather, he denies that philosophy possesses a clear idea of a simple mental substance behind particular perceptions. The lawful study of mind is therefore a study of operations: sensing, imagining, remembering, believing, desiring, fearing, loving, willing, sympathizing, judging, and inferring.

The later Enquiry preserves this methodological structure. Hume compares the study of mental operations to a “mental geography”: the philosopher may distinguish, classify, and order the powers and faculties of the mind even if their ultimate causes remain obscure. He explicitly treats distinctions such as will and understanding, imagination and passion, as real and philosophically important, while warning that deeper principles may or may not be discoverable.

This gives us the first major Humean thesis: a philosophy of mind should be a disciplined descriptive and explanatory science of mental phenomena, not a speculative science of the soul’s hidden essence.

2. The elementary inventory of mind: perceptions, impressions, and ideas

Hume’s basic term for the contents or occurrences of consciousness is “perception.” Perceptions divide into impressions and ideas. Impressions are the more forceful and lively mental appearances: sensations, passions, emotions, pains, pleasures, desires, fears, and so forth. Ideas are the fainter images of these in thinking, remembering, and reasoning. Hume also distinguishes simple and complex perceptions: a simple perception cannot be decomposed into parts, whereas a complex perception can.

This distinction is often treated as merely epistemological, but it is also Hume’s starting point in philosophy of mind. The mind is first encountered not as a bare subject but as a manifold of appearances differing in force, vivacity, affective tone, complexity, and relation. Mental life is not reducible to cool representation. It includes sensation, emotion, passion, bodily feeling, reflective feeling, memory, imagination, and volition. Hume’s mind is therefore already broader than a narrowly intellectualist conception of consciousness.

The distinction between impressions and ideas also gives Hume his famous “copy principle”: simple ideas are derived from corresponding simple impressions. In the Enquiry, he formulates the same principle by saying that the materials of thinking derive from outward or inward sentiment, and that ideas are copies of impressions. This is not a minor doctrine. It is the rule governing which alleged mental contents are intelligible. If an idea cannot be traced to some impression, sensation, passion, or inward operation, philosophy should suspect that the word is empty or confused.

A Humean philosophy of mind therefore begins with a genetic semantics of mental content. To understand an idea, ask: from what impression, feeling, operation, or combination of such materials is it derived? This applies to ordinary ideas, to metaphysical ideas, and to theological ideas.

3. The idea of God and the limits of conceiving superior minds

Hume’s copy principle does not make the idea of God meaningless. Quite the contrary: he gives a specific account of its origin. The idea of God as an infinitely intelligent, wise, and good being arises by reflecting on the operations of our own mind and augmenting the qualities of goodness and wisdom without limit. The divine mind is thus not a simple datum of experience, but neither is it a sheer nonsense-term. It is formed by analogy, reflection, enlargement, and idealization from human mental qualities.

This is crucial for the user’s concern. Hume’s remarks on God are not an external theological distraction. They belong directly to his philosophy of mind because they ask how the human mind can form an idea of a mind immeasurably superior to itself. The answer is neither crude anthropomorphism nor complete silence. We can form an idea of divine intelligence only from experienced intelligence; of divine wisdom only from experienced wisdom; of divine goodness only from experienced moral sentiment or goodness. But when those qualities are extended beyond human measure, our idea becomes analogical and increasingly indeterminate.

Hume also makes a striking concession about “other beings.” He observes that beings may possess senses of which we have no conception, because the relevant ideas have never entered us through sensation or feeling. This is one of the most important passages for a Humean treatment of angelic or superior minds. Hume’s empiricism does not entail that human sensory and mental forms exhaust all possible forms of mindedness. It entails that we cannot positively conceive mental modes for which we lack the relevant impressions.

Thus, a Humean theory of superior minds must distinguish possibility, conceivability, and evidence. It may be possible that angels, blessed spirits, or other superior intelligences possess modes of awareness inaccessible to us. But our positive idea of them must be built from materials available to human experience: intelligence, will, knowledge, affection, power, communication, obedience, moral orientation, and so on. Where those materials run out, philosophy must become modest.

4. Association: the laws of mental transition

For Hume, the mind is not a random heap of perceptions. Its ideas are connected by principles of association. The three basic associative principles are resemblance, contiguity in time or place, and cause and effect. Hume calls association a kind of “attraction” in the mental world. He is careful not to claim that he has discovered the ultimate cause of this attraction; it is an original principle of human nature known through its effects.

This is one of Hume’s most important contributions to philosophy of mind. Mental life has laws, but those laws are not always laws of reason. One idea calls up another by resemblance, by spatial or temporal nearness, or by causal relation. A portrait brings the person to mind; a room recalls past conversations; smoke suggests fire; a wound suggests pain; a facial expression suggests anger or grief.

The causal relation is the strongest associative principle because it carries the mind beyond what is present to memory and sense. In Hume’s account, much of cognition is not formal deduction but associative transition enlivened by custom. This makes Hume a precursor to later psychological accounts of cognition as habit-governed, affectively weighted, and context-sensitive.

5. Belief as a feeling or manner of conception

Hume’s account of belief is central to his philosophy of mind. Belief is not a new idea added to an imagined content. If I conceive that Caesar was assassinated and then believe that Caesar was assassinated, the representational content is not altered by adding an extra idea called “existence.” Hume explicitly says that when we affirm God to exist, we simply form the idea of such a being; existence adds no new idea to the concept. Belief changes the manner in which the idea is conceived.

Belief is therefore a felt vivacity, firmness, steadiness, or forcefulness of conception. Hume defines it in the Treatise as a lively idea related to or associated with a present impression. In the Enquiry, he again describes belief as a feeling or sentiment rather than a special idea, and emphasizes that we cannot believe at will. Belief is something the mind undergoes when certain associative and experiential conditions are satisfied.

This has large implications. Hume’s theory of belief is neither purely intellectualist nor voluntarist. Belief is not simply assent to a proposition after rational inspection, nor is it a free act of will. It is a mode of mental animation. An idea becomes belief when it is enlivened by present impressions, memory, custom, resemblance, contiguity, or causal transition.

This also explains some religious phenomena without trivializing them. Hume notes that images, relics, sensible signs, and forms of resemblance or contiguity can enliven religious ideas. The point is not merely polemical. It follows from his general theory of how absent objects become affectively and cognitively present to the mind. Religious devotion, like memory, personal attachment, patriotism, grief, and hope, involves the vivification of ideas through associative channels.

6. Causation, necessity, and the mind’s projection of connection

Hume’s account of causation is a philosophy of mind as much as a theory of knowledge. We never perceive necessary connection in a single event. We observe one event following another; after repeated conjunction, the mind forms a habit of passing from the first to the second. The impression that gives rise to the idea of necessity is not an objective glimpse of causal power but the mind’s felt determination by custom.

This applies to external causation, mental causation, bodily action, and even supposed divine causation. Hume argues that we do not perceive the power by which external objects act, nor do we perceive the power by which the will moves the body. We know from experience that bodily motion often follows volition, but the energy by which this happens escapes us. The union of soul and body is, for Hume, deeply mysterious.

The same limitation applies to the will’s command over ideas and passions. We can summon some thoughts, shift attention, or move some parts of the body, but our authority is limited and known only through experience. We cannot infer from pure reason that the will should govern the fingers but not the heart, or one passion rather than another.

Hume uses occasionalism and divine causation as serious test cases. Some philosophers, finding no intelligible power in bodies, appeal to God as the immediate cause of bodily motion, mental perception, or the union of soul and body. Hume’s reply is not contemptuous; it is methodological. If all ideas derive from impressions, and if we have no impression of causal power in bodies or minds, then appealing to divine power does not give us a clearer idea of power. We are equally ignorant of how even a supreme mind acts on body or on itself.

The result is a general Humean principle: causal necessity, whether physical, psychological, moral, or theological, is not known as a hidden tie. It is known as constant conjunction plus the mind’s customary inference. This does not mean causation is unreal in ordinary life. It means that our idea of causation is inseparable from the structure of mental expectation.

7. The soul-substance problem: why Hume rejects both dogmatic dualism and dogmatic materialism

Hume’s treatment of the soul’s immateriality is one of the most important parts of his philosophy of mind. He asks: if we have an idea of the substance of the mind, from what impression is that idea derived? Is it an impression of sensation or reflection? Is it pleasant or painful? Constant or intermittent? He finds no such impression.

If substance is defined as something capable of existing independently, then every distinct perception qualifies, because Hume holds that perceptions are distinct, separable, and capable of being conceived apart from any supposed support. That definition therefore cannot distinguish a soul-substance from particular perceptions. If substance is defined as an unknown support in which perceptions inhere, then the idea is obscure and unsupported by experience.

This is why Hume thinks the dispute over whether the soul is material or immaterial is often misframed. We have no clear idea of material substance as an ultimate support, and no clear idea of immaterial substance as an ultimate support. The question is not solved by declaring the mind material, nor by declaring it spiritual. Both claims exceed what the relevant ideas allow.

Hume’s discussion of spatial location reinforces this. Some perceptions, especially those of sight and touch, have extension. Others—passions, desires, pains, sounds, smells, moral reflections—do not occupy space in the same way. A passion is not to the right or left of a thought; a smell is not circular or square. Hume even says that many beings may exist “nowhere” in the sense of not having local position. But he does not infer from this that they inhere in a simple immaterial substance. He denies that we have a clear idea of the alleged substratum.

The consequence is subtle. Hume is not a reductive materialist. He does not say thought is nothing but matter in motion. But neither is he a Cartesian dualist. He allows that body and thought are constantly conjoined, and under his account of causation, matter and motion may be called causes of thought insofar as constant conjunction and inference are present. Yet this is an empirical causal claim, not a metaphysical reduction.

A Humean philosophy of mind therefore treats the mind-body relation as a field of observed dependence, regularity, and mutual influence, while refusing to pretend that philosophy can discern an ultimate necessary tie between body and soul.

8. The self and personal identity: bundle, theatre, republic

Hume’s famous account of personal identity follows from the same principles. When he introspects, he says he never catches himself without a perception; he encounters only heat or cold, light or shade, love or hatred, pain or pleasure. If all perceptions were removed, there would be nothing left for introspection to discover.

This yields the “bundle” account: human beings are collections or successions of perceptions in constant flux. Hume compares the mind to a theatre in which perceptions appear, pass, return, and combine, but he immediately warns that the theatre metaphor can mislead. We have no impression of a literal stage on which the perceptions are displayed, nor of the material of which such a stage is composed.

Personal identity is therefore not strict metaphysical identity. It is generated by relations among perceptions: resemblance, causation, memory, continuity of passions, and concern for past and future pains and pleasures. Hume compares the soul to a republic or commonwealth whose members change while relations, laws, and causal continuities preserve a practical identity.

Memory is especially important, but it is not the whole of identity. Memory produces resemblance among perceptions and reveals causal relations among them. It extends our sense of self backward, but we also extend identity beyond what we directly remember by causal inference: I identify myself with childhood experiences I no longer recall because they belong to a continuous chain of body, testimony, memory, character, and social recognition.

Hume’s own Appendix complicates the matter. He admits dissatisfaction with his account of personal identity. He holds two principles: first, that distinct perceptions are distinct existences; second, that the mind never perceives a real connection among distinct existences. He cannot reconcile these to his satisfaction. This is not a minor embarrassment; it is a deep aporia in Hume’s philosophy of mind. He has rejected the simple soul-substance, but he also cannot fully explain the unity of mental life from distinct perceptions alone.

The best reconstruction should therefore not present Hume’s bundle theory as a finished metaphysics. It is better understood as a skeptical-naturalistic account of how personal identity is experienced, imagined, believed, and used in life, combined with an admission that the ultimate unity of consciousness remains obscure.

9. Passions, reason, and motivation

Hume’s mind is not primarily contemplative. It is passional. He divides impressions into original impressions of sensation and secondary impressions of reflection. The latter include passions and emotions arising from prior impressions or ideas. Bodily pains and pleasures come first; grief, hope, fear, pride, humility, love, and hatred arise through reflective and associative operations.

This leads to Hume’s famous thesis that reason is, and ought only to be, the slave of the passions. The statement is often misunderstood. Hume does not mean that reasoning is worthless or that human beings should act irrationally. He means that reason alone does not generate motivation. Reason discovers relations of ideas and matters of fact; it informs us about means, consequences, probabilities, and contradictions. But motivation comes from passions, desires, aversions, affections, and sentiments.

A passion, for Hume, is an “original existence” or modification of the mind, not a representation that can be true or false in itself. A passion becomes unreasonable only indirectly: when it is founded on a false belief, or when it chooses means inadequate to its end. Correct the belief or the instrumental judgment, and the passion may change; but reason does not oppose passion as an independent motivational force.

This gives us a powerful Humean psychology. The mind is moved by affective orientations. Belief informs passion; passion directs attention; imagination vivifies objects; reason calculates relations and means. Human action is a composite of cognition and affect, but the affective component is motivationally basic.

10. Will, necessity, liberty, and moral responsibility

Hume applies his account of causation to human action. Human conduct, like physical events, exhibits regularity. We infer actions from motives, characters, circumstances, customs, education, and passions. History, politics, morals, and ordinary social life all presuppose such regularity. If human actions were not predictable from character and motive, practical life would collapse.

Necessity, in this context, does not mean a metaphysical chain perceived by reason. It means constant conjunction and inferential transition: motives, character, and circumstances are regularly conjoined with actions, and the mind passes from one to the other. This is the same kind of necessity we know in external nature.

Hume defines liberty, when applied to voluntary action, as the power of acting or not acting according to the determinations of the will. If one chooses to move and can move, or chooses to remain at rest and can remain at rest, one has liberty in the relevant sense. Liberty opposed to constraint is real and morally important; liberty opposed to necessity would be mere chance.

This is why Hume thinks necessity is essential to moral responsibility. We blame or praise actions because they reveal durable features of character, passion, and disposition. If actions were disconnected from the person’s internal principles, they would not be proper grounds for blame or praise. Hume explicitly says that actions become morally attributable when they are signs of internal character and affections.

Here Hume’s philosophy of mind becomes a philosophy of personhood. A person is not a metaphysical soul-substance, but a relatively stable organization of passions, dispositions, memories, habits, and character traits. Responsibility depends less on an occult faculty of contra-causal freedom than on whether action expresses the agent’s internal constitution.

11. Other minds, sympathy, and social cognition

Hume’s mind is not isolated. His account of sympathy is one of the most important parts of his philosophy of social mind. We do not directly perceive another person’s passion. We observe its effects: voice, gesture, expression, action, situation. From these effects we infer the passion as cause; the idea of that passion becomes lively; through sympathy it is converted into an impression in ourselves.

This means that knowledge of other minds is inferential, imaginative, causal, and affective. It is inferential because we move from signs to hidden passions. It is imaginative because we form an idea of what the other feels. It is causal because observed effects lead us to supposed mental causes. It is affective because the enlivened idea can become something like the passion itself.

Hume also emphasizes the resemblance among human minds. Because human beings share broadly similar structures of passion, imagination, and bodily expression, affections can pass from person to person. Others’ sentiments can become “in some measure” our own.

This is a profound extension of the bundle theory. The human mind is not merely a bundle of private perceptions. It is socially permeable. Its passions are shaped by sympathy, comparison, esteem, shame, pride, love, resentment, and public opinion. A Humean philosophy of mind is therefore also a philosophy of intersubjectivity.

12. Animal minds and continuity

Hume does not reserve mindedness exclusively for human beings. In his essay on immortality, he explicitly says that animals feel, think, love, hate, will, and even reason, though imperfectly. The point is philosophically important: Hume’s concept of mind is not tied to possession of a rational, immaterial soul as traditionally conceived. It is tied to observable and inferable operations: sensation, passion, volition, learning, expectation, and practical inference.

Animal minds also pressure metaphysical arguments for immortality. If thought requires an immaterial substance, and if animals think and feel, then one must either grant immaterial souls to animals or explain why human thought alone implies such a substance. Hume uses this as a challenge to metaphysical arguments, not as a denial that animals are genuinely minded.

This suggests a graded Humean philosophy of mind. Human minds, animal minds, possible angelic minds, and the divine mind are not treated by one simple criterion of substance. They are treated by operations, analogies, signs, evidence, and degrees of conceivable similarity.

13. Divine mind as the limit case of Hume’s philosophy of mind

The divine mind occupies a central and delicate place in Hume’s system. It is central because the idea of God is generated from reflection on human mental operations. It is delicate because the more God is conceived as infinitely superior to human beings, the more limited and analogical our idea becomes.

In the Dialogues concerning Natural Religion, Cleanthes argues that the world resembles a machine or human contrivance, and therefore its cause may be inferred to resemble human intelligence, though on a vastly greater scale. Demea objects that this makes the Deity too much like a human being and fails to respect divine incomprehensibility. Philo presses the skeptical point that analogical reasoning weakens as the compared cases become more dissimilar.

The final position voiced by Philo is especially relevant. He allows that the works of nature bear some analogy to the productions of human art, and that if one argues from the order of the world to a cause, one may speak of some remote analogy to mind or intelligence. But he also warns that the difference is immense, and that the resulting proposition may be ambiguous and undefined: the cause or causes of order in the universe probably bear some remote analogy to human intelligence.

For philosophy of mind, the issue is this: when may we apply the term “mind” beyond the human case? Hume’s answer is analogical. We may call something a mind when its effects resemble the effects of thought, design, intention, wisdom, or intelligence. But the legitimacy of the attribution depends on the strength of the analogy and the proportionality of the inference.

This avoids two extremes. On one side, crude anthropomorphism makes God into a magnified human mind, complete with human passions, vanities, appetites, and limitations. Hume strongly criticizes this tendency, especially in the Natural History of Religion, where he describes the human propensity to personify unknown causes and transfer human sentiments, passions, and even bodily features to invisible powers.

On the other side, if God is made wholly unlike any experienced mind, the term “mind” risks losing determinate content for us. Hume’s problem is not with reverent incomprehensibility as such; it is with the philosophical use of terms whose content has been emptied of all experiential basis. A Humean natural theology must therefore walk a narrow path: enough analogy to give content to “divine intelligence,” but enough modesty to avoid pretending that the divine mind is transparent to human categories.

Hume’s criticism of occasionalism reinforces this. If we do not understand how human will moves the body or how mind acts on matter, we do not improve matters by saying that God immediately performs every causal operation. We have no clearer impression of divine causal energy than of bodily or mental causal energy. The divine mind may be invoked theologically, but philosophy cannot claim to comprehend the manner of its operation.

Moral attributes of God present a related difficulty. In the essay on immortality, Hume warns against assuming too confidently that divine justice or merit must conform to human sentiments. Yet he also acknowledges that we have no other moral conceptions except those drawn from human sentiment. This does not refute divine moral perfection; it shows that philosophical claims about it depend on analogy and must be made cautiously.

The extracted Humean position is therefore: the divine mind is the highest and most difficult case of analogical mental attribution. It is not meaningless, because its content derives from experienced intelligence, wisdom, goodness, design, and will. It is not comprehensively knowable by natural reason, because its mode of operation, degree of perfection, and inner nature exceed impressions and ordinary analogy.

14. Angels and other created superior spirits

Hume says much less about angels than about God, but his scattered remarks allow a careful reconstruction. In the Natural History of Religion, he refers to the European belief in one supreme God who often acts by angels and subordinate ministers. He also distinguishes angels and celestial natures from lower invisible beings such as fairies, elves, and sprites. The point is not merely folkloric; Hume is analyzing how different kinds of invisible intelligent powers are conceived and classified.

For a Humean philosophy of mind, angels are best treated as possible created superior minds known, if at all, by analogy, testimony, or revelation rather than by direct philosophical inspection. The idea of an angelic mind can be formed from experienced mental materials: intelligence, volition, communication, obedience, agency, moral orientation, and perhaps superior knowledge or power. But any claim about angelic phenomenology—what it is like for an angel to perceive, remember, deliberate, or love—will be thin unless it can be tied to some impression, sign, or authoritative source.

Hume’s remark about other beings possibly possessing senses of which we have no conception applies with particular force here. If angels possess modes of awareness not introduced into human minds by sensation or reflection, then we cannot positively imagine those modes. We can only mark them as possible unknown faculties.

This makes Hume’s position neither angelological dogmatism nor dismissive reductionism. A Humean can say: the concept “angelic mind” is meaningful insofar as it is composed from intelligible mental predicates; it becomes obscure where it asserts faculties for which we have no experiential content; its evidential status depends not on introspection or ordinary observation but on testimony, revelation, or alleged effects.

Hume also warns that some controversies may lie beyond the narrow extent of human understanding, including questions about the “region of spirits.” This supports a respectful treatment: created superior spirits may be a legitimate theological topic, but natural philosophy of mind cannot anatomize their inner life with the same confidence with which it describes human passions, memory, belief, and association.

A useful Humean taxonomy would look like this. Human minds are known by introspection, conduct, sympathy, memory, and social inference. Animal minds are known by behavioral analogy and shared signs of passion and learning. Other human minds are not directly perceived but are inferred and sympathetically felt. Angelic minds are conceivable by analogy to intelligence and agency, but their special faculties are largely inaccessible to natural reason. The divine mind is the most elevated analogical case: intelligible only through reflected and augmented human mental predicates, while its ultimate operation remains incomprehensible.

15. The immortality of the soul

Hume’s treatment of immortality follows directly from his philosophy of mind. If the soul is conceived as a simple immaterial substance, Hume asks for the impression from which this idea is derived and finds none. If personal identity is a bundle or system of perceptions, then the question of survival after death becomes difficult: what survives if memory, consciousness, passion, and perception cease?

In the Treatise, Hume remarks that if death removes all perceptions, then the self would be annihilated, at least from the standpoint of what introspection can discover. This is not a demonstrative proof that no soul survives; it is an application of his principle that we have no impression of a self beyond perceptions.

In the essay “Of the Immortality of the Soul,” Hume argues that metaphysical proofs of immortality are weak. Substance, whether material or spiritual, is obscure. We cannot determine a priori what qualities matter or spirit may possess. Since cause and effect are known only by experience, we cannot prove by pure reason that matter cannot give rise to thought under some arrangement.

He also argues that even if an immaterial substance were granted, immortality of consciousness would not follow automatically. A spiritual substance might be dispersed, altered, deprived of memory, or lose its system of thought. The survival of a substrate would not by itself guarantee the survival of the person as remembered, conscious, morally concerned self.

His physical arguments are analogical: mind and body appear proportioned in weakness, vigor, disorder, and decay. Sleep, sickness, aging, and bodily injury all suggest dependence of mental life on bodily organs. Hume concludes that the analogies available to natural reason favor mortality rather than immortality.

Yet Hume’s framing is important. He writes that by the “mere light of reason” it is difficult to prove immortality, and that the gospel alone has brought life and immortality to light. Whatever one makes of Hume’s tone, the philosophical distinction is clear: natural reason does not establish the immortality of the soul; if immortality is known, it is not known through the metaphysical psychology Hume criticizes.

This is the respectful Humean position: philosophy cannot demonstrate the soul’s immortality from the idea of immaterial substance, personal identity, or divine justice. That does not amount to a philosophical anatomy of revealed doctrine. It is a limitation placed on natural reason.

16. Religion as a phenomenon of mind

Hume’s philosophy of religion is also a philosophy of mind because it analyzes how human beings form, enliven, and emotionally inhabit ideas of invisible powers. In the Natural History of Religion, he argues that human beings often respond to unknown causes with hope, fear, anxiety, gratitude, and dread. Under these passions, imagination personifies hidden powers and attributes to them intelligence, sentiment, and will.

This analysis should not be reduced to ridicule. Hume is describing a general mental mechanism: when causes are unknown but emotionally urgent, the imagination tends to model them on familiar agency. The human mind naturally transfers its own structure to unseen causes. This is the same basic mechanism by which other minds are inferred from signs, though in religion the evidence, passions, scale, and uncertainty differ.

Hume also describes a historical refinement in religious thought. Human beings may begin with limited superior powers resembling themselves, then gradually remove imperfections and elevate the nobler parts of their own frame to conceive a more perfect divinity. This is directly relevant to philosophy of mind: the concept of divine mind is not static, but formed through abstraction, purification, augmentation, and affective pressure.

At the same time, Hume criticizes the attribution of petty human passions to God. To ascribe to the Deity a restless appetite for praise or other human weaknesses risks degrading the divine into a magnified human being. In this respect, Hume’s critique of anthropomorphism can be read as a demand for theological seriousness: if divine mind is infinitely superior, philosophy must not casually project human infirmity onto it.

17. The reconstructed Humean philosophy of mind

From these materials, a comprehensive Humean philosophy of mind can be extracted in the following theses.

First, the proper object of mental philosophy is not the hidden essence of the soul but the observable and reflectively accessible operations of mind: perception, passion, belief, memory, imagination, will, association, sympathy, and judgment. Hume’s science of mind is empirical and descriptive before it is metaphysical.

Second, all intelligible ideas must be traced to impressions or to operations upon impressions. This applies to ordinary objects, the self, causal power, God, angels, and immortality. The copy principle is not merely an epistemological rule; it is the semantic discipline of Hume’s philosophy of mind.

Third, consciousness is built from perceptions, not from a directly perceived mental substance. Impressions and ideas differ in force and vivacity; impressions include sensation, emotion, passion, pain, pleasure, and inward feeling.

Fourth, the mind has lawful transitions. Resemblance, contiguity, and causation structure imagination. These principles explain memory, expectation, belief, religious association, personal identity, and social cognition.

Fifth, belief is a felt manner of conception, not a separate idea. It is vivacity, steadiness, firmness, or force added by associative and experiential conditions. This is why belief is not simply voluntary and why emotionally charged signs can enliven ideas.

Sixth, causal necessity is not perceived as an objective tie. The idea of necessity arises from the mind’s habitual transition after repeated conjunction. This applies equally to physical causation, mental causation, voluntary action, and supposed divine causation.

Seventh, the mind-body relation is empirically real but metaphysically obscure. We observe regular dependence and interaction between bodily and mental states, but we do not perceive the hidden energy by which body acts on mind or mind on body.

Eighth, the soul as simple substratum is not an object of clear philosophical knowledge. Hume’s critique undermines both confident materialism and confident immaterialism when they are framed as doctrines about ultimate substance.

Ninth, the self is a practical, memorial, causal, and imaginative unity rather than a strictly identical substance. Personal identity arises from relations among perceptions, especially memory, resemblance, causation, concern, and continuity of character.

Tenth, Hume’s own doubts about personal identity must remain part of the system. The unity of distinct perceptions is not finally explained. This prevents the Humean philosophy of mind from becoming a dogmatic “bundle metaphysics.”

Eleventh, passions are central mental realities. Reason informs, compares, calculates, and corrects beliefs about means, but passion supplies motivation. Human agency is affectively grounded.

Twelfth, liberty and necessity are compatible when properly defined. Necessity is regular conjunction and inference; liberty is acting according to the determinations of the will rather than under external constraint. Moral responsibility depends on actions expressing durable features of character.

Thirteenth, other minds are known through signs, inference, and sympathy. Human beings do not directly perceive one another’s passions, but they infer and internally reproduce them through imagination and affective contagion.

Fourteenth, nonhuman minds are philosophically significant. Animals feel, will, love, hate, and reason imperfectly. Mindedness is therefore graded and operational, not confined to a metaphysical human soul-substance.

Fifteenth, superior created minds, including angels, are possible objects of analogical conception but not detailed natural knowledge. We can conceive them through intelligence, will, agency, and moral relation; we cannot positively conceive faculties for which we have no impressions.

Sixteenth, the divine mind is the limit case of mental attribution. Its idea is formed by augmenting human mental qualities, but natural reason can establish at most a remote analogy between the cause of order and human intelligence. Stronger claims require a source beyond ordinary philosophical inference.

Seventeenth, immortality cannot be demonstrated by Hume’s natural philosophy of mind. Arguments from immaterial substance, moral desert, or metaphysical necessity fail to establish survival of personal consciousness. Hume leaves room for the distinct category of revelation, but not for philosophical proof from the mere concept of soul.

18. What Hume is not saying

Hume is not saying that consciousness is unreal. He begins from perceptions as the most immediate realities available to reflection.

He is not saying that the soul is disproved in the way an empirical hypothesis might be disproved. Rather, he argues that philosophy lacks a clear idea of a simple, identical soul-substance apart from perceptions.

He is not a straightforward materialist. He permits body-thought causal regularities, but he denies that we understand the ultimate substance or necessary power of either matter or spirit.

He is not a simple behaviorist. Other minds are inferred from behavior, but sympathy transforms ideas of others’ passions into felt impressions; inner life is real and central.

He is not merely dismissing God or angels. Divine and angelic minds are serious boundary cases for his theory of ideas, analogy, causation, and mental attribution. What he denies is not their theological dignity, but the ability of unaided natural philosophy to comprehend their inner nature or causal power.

He is not offering a fully settled theory of personal identity. His Appendix shows that he recognizes a serious unresolved difficulty in explaining mental unity without either a perceived real connection or a simple substance.

19. Final synthesis

Hume’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a disciplined empiricism of mental life. Its primitive data are perceptions. Its basic dynamics are association, vivacity, custom, passion, sympathy, and causal inference. Its account of the self is practical and relational rather than substantial. Its account of agency is compatibilist and character-based. Its account of other minds is inferential and sympathetic. Its account of God and angels is analogical, respectful of possible superiority, but rigorously constrained by the limits of human ideas.

The soul, in Hume, is not eliminated so much as transformed as a philosophical object. The soul as an unknowable support behind perceptions cannot be made clear by natural reason. The soul as a living order of perceptions, passions, memories, beliefs, habits, affections, and concerns is the central object of the science of man. The divine mind and angelic minds stand at the edge of this science: conceivable through analogy with human mindedness, perhaps knowable through non-philosophical sources if such sources are granted, but not anatomizable by the ordinary methods of empirical philosophy.

The deepest Humean lesson is therefore one of disciplined proportion. Speak confidently where impressions, operations, and observed regularities support us. Speak analogically where resemblance supports but does not demonstrate. Speak modestly where the subject is superior mind, divine operation, angelic faculty, or immortality. And where the mind reaches the edge of its own light, do not confuse obscurity with refutation, or reverence with knowledge.

PreviousThomas Reid NextMary Shepherd

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
